author: raxhvl
content:
> I think I assumed adding it to access list is different notion from adding it to BAL. If they are same then than is great!

BAL is a much truer representation of state access and should not be coupled with the warm set.

The warm set is much more liberal and includes: untouched precompiles, coinbase (even for a zero tip), and untouched 2929 addresses.
BAL includes them only if touched by a transaction, and warm set include address that are *likely* to be accessed in a transaction, even if they ultimately are not.

The reverse holds too. System contracts touched during pre/post-execution show up in BAL. Since the warm set is scoped to transaction execution, they are naturally excluded.

I don't think we should worry too much about syncing the warm set and BAL, as they already diverge quite a bit.

author: nero_eth
content:
Do they actually diverge in both directions?
E.g. with the change that dragan proposes, the BAL would become a strict subset of the wam/cold access (not including things that are warm for free such as precompiles)

author: draganrakita
content: I would propose clients to implement this solution as it is faster and performance matters.

author: omerfirmak
content:
> I don't think we should worry too much about syncing the warm set and BAL

I don't think that is what is being proposed here. Only the `CALL*` opcodes' interactions with BAL and warm set is getting further specified. No?

author: raxhvl
content: EIP-2930  optional access list is one case i can think of , (not free but charged per item AND transaction scoped). If the warmed address is NOT accessed by tx then BAL is not a strict subset of warm set

author: nero_eth
content: right but 2930 itself is treated as an edge case wrt BALs

author: nero_eth
content: Apart from that, there shouldn't be any such case

author: raxhvl
content: I'll double check to see if i can find anything else. Im for the performance gain by making BAL a subset. It quite logical.

author: raxhvl
content: <@429719902363058177> Curious how are you handling this eip-2930 diversion in your code

author: draganrakita
content: Access list is accessed only if account is cold and needs to be warmed. If it is already warmed it is part of main state map. I needed to add separate HashMap so it is not included in BAL, previously all access list were loaded directly to main state map.

author: nero_eth
content: <@&1460290804898861117> pls chime in on this one (see replied to msg). Best, we get to agreement soon, otherwise, we go with not merging it and keeping things as-is.

author: raxhvl
content: I have combed through the EELS implementation, I found these differences.

author: raxhvl
content: (This is except the 7702 delegate we are discussing, which is currently number 4 on the left)

author: draganrakita
content: I don't understand the system addresses part, this looks like impl specific as no gas is stored in system calls right?

author: raxhvl
content:
So this was meant to show the difference between access list and BAL at block level. Both are set of addresses.

On the left is set of all addresses from the access list for all txs in the block. (Union of individual tx access list)

On the right is set of all addresses  from BAL for the same block.

Not saying anything about implementation, just that BAL records system address while access list does not (naturally because access list are bounded to user txs).


Access list on the other hand has some surplus pre-warmed entries which were not actually read from disk, and thus does not end up in BAL.

Does that makes sense?

author: draganrakita
content: Imo, bal is subset of access list, hence my comment about system addresses, it looks that you saying that it is implementation specific, and not bounded by protocol.
